questions.question: A card pays $10 if it is a heart, else $0. The dealer reveals the card is red (suit class shown, rank hidden). Conditional fair value is:

explanations.A: Correct. Given red, half the red cards are hearts: P(heart|red) = 1/2, so EV = $5.
explanations.B: $2.5 is the unconditional EV (P(heart)=1/4); the red clue updates it.
explanations.C: $10 is the max payout; not every red card is a heart.
explanations.D: $0.769 uses P=1/13, which ignores that suits split the deck into quarters.

questions.question: A contract pays the number of heads among 3 fair coins. So far 2 show heads and 0 show tails; 1 remain hidden. Fair value is:

explanations.B: Correct. Known heads 2 + E[hidden] = 2 + 0.5×1 = 2.5.
explanations.A: 2 counts only the revealed heads and ignores the 1 hidden coins (each worth 0.5).
explanations.C: 3 assumes every hidden coin is a head; each is only 0.5 in expectation.
explanations.D: 1.5 is the prior EV before any reveal; the shown faces are now fixed.

questions.question: Fair value is 50 and you are long 3 (position limit +4) after repeated lifts of nothing — your bid kept getting hit. To manage inventory you should:
options: Skew quotes down — lower bid and ask so selling your inventory is easier and buying more is less likely

explanations.D: Symmetric widening does not address the directional need to reduce a long.

questions.question: You quote 14 at 16. An aggressive, likely-informed trader immediately lifts your ask. The best inference and response is:
options: They think fair is below your bid
Flow is random; ignore it and never update
Your spread is simply too wide
They value the contract above your ask; consider raising your quotes

explanations.D: Correct. A trader lifting your ask reveals they think fair is above 16; informed buy flow says lift your market up.
explanations.A: If fair were below 14 they would sell to your bid, not buy your ask.
explanations.B: Aggressive, immediate fills are usually directional information, not noise.
explanations.C: Spread width does not explain a one-sided aggressive lift.

questions.question: You are very confident in your fair-value estimate and volatility is low. Relative to a baseline, you should quote a spread that is:

explanations.C: Correct. High confidence and low volatility mean less adverse-selection risk, so you can quote tighter to win more flow.
explanations.A: Widening is for uncertainty/volatility, not for high confidence.
explanations.B: A crossed market (bid above ask) is always invalid.
explanations.D: You still quote two-sided; a tighter spread is not a zero one-sided quote.

questions.question: Your fair-value estimate is shaky and the payoff is highly volatile. Relative to a baseline, you should quote a spread that is:

explanations.D: Correct. Greater uncertainty and volatility raise adverse-selection risk, so widen to protect against being picked off.
explanations.A: Tightening under uncertainty invites informed traders to pick you off.
explanations.B: A crossed market is invalid regardless of confidence.
explanations.C: Spread should respond to uncertainty; ignoring it is poor risk control.

questions.question: Many informed-looking traders are active and news may drop any second. Your spread should be:
options: Wider, and ready to re-center on news
As tight as possible to win flow

explanations.A: Correct. Toxic flow plus pending news means high adverse selection; widen and update fast.
explanations.B: Tight quotes against informed flow lose to adverse selection.
explanations.C: You generally maintain a two-sided market; width, not sidedness, manages this risk.
explanations.D: Crossing is invalid and guarantees losses.

questions.question: Fair was 50; you are flat with a 49 at 51 quote. The moderator announces public news that lifts fair to 55. Before any new fills, your best action is:
options: Keep 49 at 51 since spread width is fine
Re-center to about 54 at 56, then resume quoting
Stop quoting for the rest of the game

explanations.B: Correct. Update fair to 55 first, then place a symmetric spread around it; the old 49 at 51 is now stale and would be picked off.
explanations.A: Width is irrelevant when the center is 5 off; every buyer lifts your cheap 51.
explanations.C: Skewing down contradicts the news that raised fair; you'd sell cheap.
explanations.D: Withdrawing forfeits edge; the correct move is to re-center and continue.

questions.question: Fair 30, you are long 4 at a +5 limit, and a public clue just lowered fair to 27. Best next quote?
options: Re-center down near 26 at 28 and lean to sell

explanations.C: Correct. Fair dropped to 27 and you are long, so both the news and inventory point the same way: shade down and prioritize selling.
explanations.A: Raising fights both the bearish news and your need to reduce a long.
explanations.B: Stale center above the new 27 fair invites buyers to lift you while you're already long.
explanations.D: Adding length near the limit against bearish news compounds risk.

questions.question: Fair 100, you are short 3 (−4 limit). A trader keeps hitting your bid (you keep buying). This flow plus your short means you should:
options: Raise your bid to buy even faster
Flip to a long position deliberately
Welcome the buys to cover, but watch that repeated hits may signal fair is dropping

explanations.D: Correct. Buying covers your short (good for inventory), yet persistent sell pressure can mean fair is falling, so re-evaluate fair before quoting again.
explanations.A: Chasing fills ignores the adverse-selection signal in repeated one-sided hits.
explanations.B: Persistent directional flow is information you must weigh.
explanations.C: Overshooting from short to long adds unnecessary directional risk.

questions.question: You flip a fair coin until it shows heads, and the contract pays the total number of flips made. Fair value is:

explanations.C: Correct. Flips until the first heads is geometric with p=1/2, so E = 1/p = 2.
explanations.A: 1 would mean it always stops on the first flip; with p=1/2 the mean is 2.
explanations.B: 0.5 is the per-flip success probability, not the expected number of flips.
explanations.D: 4 over-counts; E[geometric, p=1/2] = 2 exactly.
